age_groups.title: Preschool
age_groups.guidance: Learn through repetition, gestures, pictures, and a grown-up helper.

age_groups.title: Kids
age_groups.guidance: Learn through short role-play, drawing, matching, and speaking aloud.

age_groups.title: Preteen
age_groups.guidance: Use real-life scenarios, memory challenges, and short conversations.

age_groups.title: Teen
age_groups.guidance: Practice natural conversation, independence, culture, and confident speaking.

age_groups.title: Adult
age_groups.guidance: Build practical language for everyday life, work, travel, and relationships.

modules.title: Greetings
modules.description: Meet people, introduce yourself, and close conversations with confidence.

lessons.title: Hello and Goodbye
lessons.teaching: Use bonjour during the day with friends, teachers, coworkers, and new people.
lessons.practice: Greet three imaginary people with a smile and say bonjour clearly.
lessons.culture: A greeting is expected when entering many shops and small businesses in French-speaking places.

lessons.title: My Name Is
lessons.teaching: Je m'appelle literally introduces the name you are called.
lessons.practice: Replace Alex with your name and introduce yourself three times.
lessons.culture: First names are common among peers; titles are polite in formal settings.

lessons.title: How Are You?
lessons.teaching: Use allez-vous for a polite or formal conversation.
lessons.practice: Ask the question, pause, and imagine listening to the answer.
lessons.culture: Ça va ? is the shorter casual version used with friends and family.

lessons.title: I Am Well
lessons.teaching: Je vais bien is a natural positive response to a greeting question.
lessons.practice: Answer once slowly, then once at normal conversation speed.
lessons.culture: Adding merci makes the response warm and courteous.

lessons.title: Please and Thank You
lessons.teaching: These two courtesy expressions make requests and responses polite.
lessons.practice: Pretend to request an object, then thank the person who gives it to you.
lessons.culture: Polite language is especially important with strangers and service workers.

lessons.title: Excuse Me
lessons.teaching: Use excusez-moi to get attention, pass someone, or begin a question politely.
lessons.practice: Say it before asking an imaginary person for directions.
lessons.culture: Pardon is also common for a quick apology in a crowded place.

lessons.title: Nice to Meet You
lessons.teaching: Enchanté is said by a man and enchantée by a woman, though they sound the same.
lessons.practice: Introduce yourself, then finish with enchanté or enchantée.
lessons.culture: A handshake is traditional in formal introductions; customs vary by setting.

lessons.title: Where Are You From?
lessons.teaching: D'où asks from where, and venez-vous keeps the question polite.
lessons.practice: Ask the question and point to a map or an imagined hometown.
lessons.culture: This question can open a friendly conversation about regions and cultures.

lessons.title: I Come From
lessons.teaching: Use je viens de, du, or des depending on the place that follows.
lessons.practice: Replace Canada with your country or city and say the complete sentence.
lessons.culture: French place names may use a gendered article that changes de to du or de la.

lessons.title: See You Soon
lessons.teaching: À bientôt closes a conversation when you expect to meet again soon.
lessons.practice: Wave and say the phrase naturally as if leaving a friend.
lessons.culture: À demain means see you tomorrow; au revoir is the general goodbye.

modules.title: Food
modules.description: Order meals, shop for ingredients, and talk about tastes and hunger.

lessons.title: Ask for Water
lessons.teaching: Je voudrais is a courteous way to request food or drink.
lessons.practice: Hold an imaginary glass and make the request politely.
lessons.culture: Tap water may be requested as une carafe d'eau in a restaurant.

lessons.title: Buy Bread
lessons.teaching: Use une because baguette is a feminine noun.
lessons.practice: Pretend to enter a bakery, greet the baker, and order one baguette.
lessons.culture: Greeting with bonjour before ordering is an important bakery custom.

lessons.title: Read the Menu
lessons.teaching: Puis-je is a formal way to ask may I.
lessons.practice: Ask for the menu, then scan an imaginary list of dishes.
lessons.culture: Le menu can mean a set-price meal as well as the menu itself.

lessons.title: Say What You Like
lessons.teaching: Use j'aime plus a noun to share a food you enjoy.
lessons.practice: Replace apples with three foods and speak each sentence.
lessons.culture: French usually uses an article such as le, la, or les when discussing foods generally.

lessons.title: Say What You Dislike
lessons.teaching: Ne and pas surround the verb to make the sentence negative.
lessons.practice: Name one food you do not like, while keeping your tone respectful.
lessons.culture: In casual speech, speakers may soften dislikes with je préfère, meaning I prefer.

lessons.title: I Am Hungry
lessons.teaching: French uses have hunger rather than be hungry.
lessons.practice: Say j'ai faim, then name what you would like to eat.
lessons.culture: J'ai soif follows the same pattern and means I am thirsty.

lessons.title: Breakfast
lessons.teaching: Prendre is commonly used for having a meal.
lessons.practice: Describe having breakfast and name one breakfast item.
lessons.culture: A traditional breakfast may be light, with bread, pastry, coffee, or hot chocolate.

lessons.title: At the Market
lessons.teaching: Combien asks how much and ceci points to this item.
lessons.practice: Point to three imaginary market items and ask the price.
lessons.culture: Open-air markets are social places where a greeting comes before a question.

lessons.title: It Is Delicious
lessons.teaching: C'est means it is and introduces a description.
lessons.practice: Taste an imaginary dish and respond with enthusiasm.
lessons.culture: Complimenting a host or cook is a warm way to show appreciation.

lessons.title: Ask for the Bill
lessons.teaching: L'addition is the restaurant bill; request it when you are ready to leave.
lessons.practice: Finish an imaginary meal and ask your server for the bill.
lessons.culture: The bill is not always brought until requested, allowing guests time to talk.

modules.title: Transportation & Travel
modules.description: Move through cities and trips using buses, trains, airports, hotels, and directions.

lessons.title: Find the Bus Stop
lessons.teaching: Où est asks where a single place is located.
lessons.practice: Ask the question and follow an imaginary pointing direction.
lessons.culture: Bus stops often display route numbers and arrival information.

lessons.title: Buy a Train Ticket
lessons.french: Un billet pour Paris, s'il vous plaît.
lessons.pronunciation: uhn bee-yay poor pah-ree seel voo play
lessons.teaching: Un billet pour names the destination of a ticket.
lessons.practice: Replace Paris with another city and order the ticket.
lessons.culture: Validate or scan tickets when required before or during the trip.

lessons.title: Take the Metro
lessons.teaching: Prendre is used for taking public transportation.
lessons.practice: Say how you travel, then add a destination.
lessons.culture: Metro signs may use sortie for exit and correspondance for transfer.

lessons.title: Call a Taxi
lessons.french: Appelez un taxi, s'il vous plaît.
lessons.pronunciation: ah-puh-lay uhn tak-see seel voo play
lessons.teaching: Appelez is a polite command used when asking someone to call.
lessons.practice: Make the request and add where you need to go.
lessons.culture: Official taxi stands are common near stations and airports.

lessons.title: Ask for Directions
lessons.english: How do I get to the station?
lessons.teaching: Comment aller à asks how to reach a destination.
lessons.practice: Ask, then repeat left, right, and straight gestures.
lessons.culture: La gare usually means a train station; une station may refer to metro stops.

lessons.title: At the Airport
lessons.teaching: The vowel sounds join in l'aéroport for smooth pronunciation.
lessons.practice: Ask the question, then name your departure time.
lessons.culture: Départs means departures and arrivées means arrivals.

lessons.title: Show Your Passport
lessons.teaching: Voici presents something that is close at hand.
lessons.practice: Hold an imaginary passport and present it confidently.
lessons.culture: Keep travel documents accessible but secure during checkpoints.

lessons.title: Hotel Reservation
lessons.teaching: Use j'ai to state that you have a booking.
lessons.practice: Add your name after the phrase as if checking into a hotel.
lessons.culture: A polite bonjour begins most hotel desk conversations.

lessons.title: Departure Time
lessons.english: What time does the train leave?
lessons.french: À quelle heure part le train ?
lessons.pronunciation: ah kel uhr par luh tran
lessons.teaching: À quelle heure asks at what time an event happens.
lessons.practice: Ask the question and answer with an imaginary time.
lessons.culture: The 24-hour clock is common on schedules in French-speaking regions.

lessons.title: We Have Arrived
lessons.teaching: Arriver uses être in the completed past form.
lessons.practice: Say the phrase with relief, then name the destination.
lessons.culture: Bienvenue means welcome and may greet you at your destination.

modules.title: Ocean
modules.description: Explore beaches, weather, boats, safety, and care for marine life.

lessons.title: Go to the Beach
lessons.english: Let us go to the beach.
lessons.teaching: Allons is the let us form of aller, to go.
lessons.practice: Invite someone and point toward an imaginary beach.
lessons.culture: La plage can describe sandy, pebble, lake, or seaside beaches.

lessons.title: Describe the Ocean
lessons.teaching: Est connects the ocean to the description magnifique.
lessons.practice: Look at an ocean image and say the sentence with expression.
lessons.culture: French-speaking coastlines span the Atlantic, Pacific, Indian Ocean, and Caribbean.

lessons.title: I Can Swim
lessons.teaching: Savoir plus an infinitive describes knowing how to do something.
lessons.practice: Say the phrase and mime a safe swimming stroke.
lessons.culture: Swimming conditions and safety flags vary by beach.

lessons.title: Strong Waves
lessons.teaching: The plural adjective fortes agrees with les vagues.
lessons.practice: Say the warning clearly as if helping a friend stay safe.
lessons.culture: Respecting posted flags and lifeguards is part of beach safety.

lessons.title: Board the Boat
lessons.french: Nous montons à bord du bateau.
lessons.pronunciation: noo mon-ton ah bor dew bah-toh
lessons.teaching: À bord means aboard a vehicle or vessel.
lessons.practice: Pretend to step aboard while saying the complete sentence.
lessons.culture: Bateau is a general word for boat; navire often means a larger ship.

lessons.title: Wear a Life Jacket
lessons.teaching: Mettez gives a polite or plural safety instruction.
lessons.practice: Mime fastening a jacket while giving the instruction.
lessons.culture: Safety language should be short, clear, and followed immediately.

lessons.title: Ask About Weather
lessons.teaching: This fixed expression asks about current weather.
lessons.practice: Ask the question, then answer sunny, windy, or rainy in English for now.
lessons.culture: Coastal weather can change quickly, so forecasts matter before boating.

lessons.title: Collect Seashells
lessons.teaching: Ramasser means to pick up or collect from the ground.
lessons.practice: Count imaginary shells as you repeat the sentence.
lessons.culture: Some protected beaches ask visitors to leave shells and wildlife in place.

lessons.title: See the Fish
lessons.teaching: Regardez directs one person politely or several people to look.
lessons.practice: Point below the water and say the phrase excitedly.
lessons.culture: Poisson is singular; poissons is plural, though the final s is silent.

lessons.title: Protect the Ocean
lessons.teaching: Protégeons invites a group to take action together.
lessons.practice: Name one action, such as reducing plastic, after saying the phrase.
lessons.culture: Francophone communities worldwide participate in marine conservation.

modules.title: Sports
modules.description: Talk about games, teams, practice, movement, scores, and fair play.

lessons.title: Play Soccer
lessons.teaching: Jouer à is used with most sports and games.
lessons.practice: Name the sport and mime passing a ball.
lessons.culture: Football usually means soccer in French; American football is football américain.

lessons.title: Watch the Match
lessons.teaching: Regardons invites others to watch together.
lessons.practice: Invite a friend and point toward an imaginary screen or field.
lessons.culture: Match is a borrowed word used widely in French sports conversation.

lessons.title: My Team
lessons.teaching: Mon is used before équipe because the word begins with a vowel sound.
lessons.practice: Point to a team photo or imagined group and introduce them.
lessons.culture: Équipe describes a team in sports, work, and group projects.

lessons.title: Ask the Score
lessons.teaching: Quel est asks what is when the answer is a specific value.
lessons.practice: Ask the question, then answer with two numbers.
lessons.culture: Scores may be announced with à between the two numbers.

lessons.title: We Won
lessons.teaching: Avons gagné is the completed past form of winning.
lessons.practice: Celebrate respectfully and say the sentence once.
lessons.culture: Good sportsmanship matters whether a team wins or loses.

lessons.title: Training Today
lessons.teaching: S'entraîner means to train or practice for a skill.
lessons.practice: Name one exercise you will do after the phrase.
lessons.culture: Entraînement means a practice or training session.

lessons.title: Run Fast
lessons.teaching: Cours is the present form of courir for je.
lessons.practice: Say the phrase, then walk or run safely in place.
lessons.culture: Course can mean a race, while courir is the verb to run.

lessons.title: Go Swimming
lessons.teaching: Faire de is used for many activities and sports.
lessons.practice: Mime a stroke and say the phrase at a steady pace.
lessons.culture: Natation refers to swimming as a sport or activity.

lessons.title: Fair Play
lessons.teaching: Bien joué praises a good effort, move, or performance.
lessons.practice: Congratulate both a teammate and an opponent.
lessons.culture: The phrase works in sports, games, and many everyday successes.

lessons.title: Ready to Play
lessons.teaching: Prêt or prête agrees with the speaker, though the final sound may differ slightly.
lessons.practice: Stand ready, say the phrase, and begin an imaginary game.
lessons.culture: À vous means your turn and can begin friendly play.
